topic: phone is acting up — asking for troubleshooting help

turns.content: My phone is doing something infuriating. The screen goes black for two or three seconds in the middle of doing things and then comes back like nothing happened. It has been doing this all week. What is wrong with it?

That sounds like it could be a few different things. Let me narrow it down:

1. **What kind of phone?** iPhone or Android, and do you know the model?
2. **Does it happen during specific apps, or randomly during anything?**
3. **Has the phone been dropped recently or exposed to water?**
4. **How old is the phone?**

The black screen flicker could be a software issue, a display connection problem, or a battery-related shutdown. The answers to those questions will help me point you in the right direction.

It is an iPhone. I do not know the model number — Carlos set it up for me. It is maybe two years old. I have not dropped it. It happens randomly — I could be reading email, looking at photos, anything. The screen just goes dark and then comes back. It is like the phone is blinking.

The blinking behavior on a two-year-old iPhone is most commonly one of these:

**Most likely: Software glitch**
- iOS can develop display bugs, especially after updates. Try these steps first:
  1. **Force restart**: Press and quickly release the volume up button, then the volume down button, then press and hold the side button until the Apple logo appears. This clears minor software issues.
  2. **Check for an iOS update**: Go to Settings > General > Software Update. If there's a pending update, install it — Apple frequently patches display bugs.

**Second possibility: Auto-brightness or True Tone acting up**
- Go to Settings > Accessibility > Display & Text Size, and toggle off "Auto-Brightness" at the bottom. Also try Settings > Display & Brightness and toggle off "True Tone." Sometimes the ambient light sensor misbehaves and causes the screen to flicker.

**Less likely but possible: Hardware issue**
- If the screen is physically separating from the frame even slightly, or if the battery is swelling, that can cause intermittent display blackouts. Look at the phone from the side — is the screen flush with the edges or is there any gap?

Start with the force restart and let me know if it helps.

I am going to try the force restart. Hold on. Volume up, volume down, then hold the side button. All right, the Apple logo appeared. It is restarting. If I were not a patient woman this phone would have gone through a window three days ago.

Your restraint is noted and admired. While it restarts, here's what to watch for:

- If the blackout stops happening after the restart, it was likely a software glitch and you're done.
- If it happens again within the next day or two, we move to the auto-brightness and True Tone settings.
- If it persists after that, it's probably hardware and you'd want to take it to an Apple Store or an authorized repair shop.

Give it a little time after the restart before testing it heavily — sometimes the phone needs a few minutes to settle after a force restart.

It is back on. Everything looks normal. Now let me ask you something else while I have you — my storage is apparently almost full. I keep getting that notification. I take too many pictures, I know. But I am not deleting my photos. Is there another way?

You don't have to delete anything. There are a few ways to free up storage while keeping every photo:

**1. Optimize iPhone Storage (easiest):**
- Go to Settings > Photos > toggle on "Optimize iPhone Storage"
- This keeps full-resolution photos in iCloud and stores smaller versions on your phone. When you want to look at a photo, it downloads the full version on demand. You won't notice any difference in daily use.
- This requires iCloud storage. Go to Settings > [your name] > iCloud > Manage Account Storage to check your plan. If you're on the free 5GB, you'll need to upgrade — the 200GB plan is $2.99/month and is probably enough.

**2. Clear other storage hogs:**
- Go to Settings > General > iPhone Storage. It will show you which apps are using the most space. Common culprits: Messages (old text threads with photos and videos), podcasts, and cached data from apps you rarely use.
- You can offload apps you don't use — this removes the app but keeps its data, so if you reinstall it later, everything comes back.

**3. Check "Recently Deleted":**
- In the Photos app, scroll down to "Recently Deleted." Photos you've deleted in the last 30 days are still taking up space there. Empty that album.

The Optimize iPhone Storage option alone will probably solve your problem without you having to change anything about how you use the phone.

The recently deleted folder. I did not know that existed. Let me check. Dios mio — there are hundreds of photos in here. Screenshots, duplicates, blurry ones. This has been taking up space the entire time?

Yes, exactly. When you delete a photo, the iPhone keeps it in that folder for 30 days in case you change your mind. If you never empty it, those photos sit there consuming storage until the 30-day window expires on each one individually.

You can tap "Select" in the top right corner, then "Delete All" in the bottom left to clear the entire folder at once. That should free up a significant amount of space immediately.

After you clear that, check your storage again at Settings > General > iPhone Storage. It may take a minute to recalculate. If you're still tight on space, we can look at the iCloud optimization option.

I just deleted all of them. Two hundred and forty-three photos. I feel like I just cleaned out a closet. The storage notification is gone already. Why does Apple make this so unnecessarily complicated? I went to law school and I need a tutorial to manage my own phone.

In fairness, law school taught you to interpret statutes, not Apple's design philosophy — though sometimes they're equally opaque. The Recently Deleted folder is one of those features that Apple added to protect people from accidental deletion but never made obvious enough for people to know it needs maintenance.

Now that you've cleared it, one habit that will keep this from building up again: once a month, open the Recently Deleted album and clear it. Or if you want to skip the safety net entirely, you can go to Settings > Photos > and look for an option to reduce the deletion window. But honestly, a monthly clearing takes ten seconds.

How's the screen? Any blackouts since the restart?

No blackouts so far. I will give it a day and see. If it starts again I will come back and we will try the brightness settings. Now, while we are at it — Carlos keeps telling me I should set up my phone so my photos save to the cloud automatically. He says if the phone breaks I will lose everything. Is he right?

Carlos is absolutely right. If your photos are only on the phone and the phone is damaged, lost, or stolen, those photos are gone. iCloud Photos is the easiest solution since you're on an iPhone:

- Go to Settings > [your name] > iCloud > Photos
- Toggle on "iCloud Photos"
- This automatically uploads every photo and video to iCloud as a backup

If you're still on the free 5GB plan, you'll almost certainly need to upgrade. The 200GB plan at $2.99 a month is the sweet spot for most people. Given the hundreds of photos you just cleared from the Recently Deleted folder, you clearly take a lot of pictures — the peace of mind is worth three dollars.

Once it's on, you never have to think about it again. Every photo backs up automatically over Wi-Fi.

Three dollars a month to never lose a picture of my granddaughter. Claro, that is nothing. I will do it tonight. Carlos will be very pleased that I finally listened to him — he has been saying this for a year. I will not tell him you were the one who convinced me. Let him think I came around on my own.

Your secret is safe. And once it's set up, you can also access all your photos from any device — your iPad, a computer, anything signed into your Apple ID. So even if the phone goes through the window next time, every picture is safe in the cloud.

Glad we got the screen fixed and the storage sorted in one session. If the blackouts return, the auto-brightness toggle is the next step — you know where to find me. Good luck with the phone.
